comments.post_body: I’m happy to share that I’m starting a new position as Software Development Engineer at Amazon Web Services (AWS)!
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about someone starting a new position as a Software Development Engineer at Amazon Web Services (AWS), likely offering congratulations or discussing the opportunity. The conversation is centered around this new career development and the commenter's reaction to the news.

comments.comment_body: Bill Karwin I thought you, "... preferred not to practice the same bad behavior that they did."?
comments.post_body:
The worst interviewer I ever had:

He swaggered into the room and sat down heavily in his chair. “I just have one question for you,” were his first words. “What’s the difference between Java and C++?”

I described some differences, like garbage collection and generics, and the fact that Java is a proprietary language whereas C++ is an ANSI standard, Java compilers generate bytecode, whereas C++ compilers usually generate native machine code, and so on.

“WRONG!” he roared. “There’s no difference! They’re exactly the same language!” Then he sat back, obviously satisfied at having demonstrated his superiority.

I wanted to ask him, “What did you think you would learn about me as a candidate by asking that question?”

Or, “If I accept a job at this company, would I ever have to talk to you again?”

But I just nodded pleasantly and waited for him to pass me to the next interviewer. I did not complain to the company about him. What would be the point? Did I hope to persuade the company to fire him somehow? If I still wanted the job (which is doubtful), would complaining about an interviewer be the best way to make that happen?

If you have a bad feeling about an interviewer, assume that working for that person will be terrible. Politely finish the interview and never look back.

Complaining about an arrogant interviewer has virtually no upside for you, and is much more likely to demonstrate that you’re just as big an asshole as the interviewer was.
comments.context_summary: The post describes a horrific job interview experience with an arrogant and misinformed interviewer who claimed Java and C++ are the same language, and the commenter is responding to this story by likely sharing their own thoughts on the matter. The post warns against complaining about bad interviewers, as it's unlikely to yield positive results and may reflect poorly on the candidate.

comments.comment_body: Best of luck, although, I know you won't need it Arda!!!
comments.post_body:
This week, Friday, was my last day of employment at Microsoft.

I am leaving the company to try something different... both in terms of the domain and the size of the company. Although I am super excited for the adventure and experience coming along my way, I do have mixed feelings, as well, because Microsoft has helped me grow tremendously, both in my career and as a person in general, with its culture built on the following pillars.

**Customer Obsession.** We always listen to our customers and try to understand their needs, and we prioritize our work accordingly. We create impact by not only shipping features that directly meet our customers' needs but also contributing to others' success via, e.g., open-source projects and conferences. During my tenure, I have had the privilege and honor to lead two important projects, among others, both of which got presented by Satya Nadella himself in his keynotes at Microsoft Build conferences. Likewise, I have had the opportunity to contribute to open-source projects and present our work in different international PostgreSQL conferences and events in the US and Europe.

**Growth Mindset, Diverse and Inclusive, One Microsoft.** For me, these pillars of the Microsoft culture have been the most transformational. Coming from a non-CS background, I managed to be part of the Azure Database for PostgreSQL team. Thanks to the support of our leadership team, I was admitted to Azure Data University, an internal program to help engineers upskill themselves in the state-of-the-art database research and technologies, and upon successfully graduating from the program, I had the privilege to participate as an assistant in the next cohort of the program. Similarly, my managers also provided great support (and flexibility when I needed) for me to obtain an executive MBA degree during my tenure.

Of course, it was not only roses. There were a lot of times when I needed to put my perseverance and resilience to the test. In the end, however, I believe I have become a stronger professional and individual.

As I depart from Microsoft, I would like to thank, once again, all my former and current managers, the leadership team, our product managers and all my colleagues for providing such a great and supportive environment.

#MicrosoftAlumni
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about the author's last day at Microsoft, where they reflect on their time at the company and the skills they've gained, and are now leaving to try something new. The author expresses mixed feelings, being excited for the new adventure but also grateful for the growth and experiences they had at Microsoft.

comments.comment_body: I would like to call out Muhammad Taha Naveed for his tireless work creating, testing, and reviewing PRs. I greatly appreciate all of his work and he deserves a big thank you from all of us too!
comments.post_body:
📢 AGE, the PostgreSQL extension that transforms it into a graph database, has just released version 1.7.0 with support for PG18.

A big thank you 🙏 to the entire AGE open-source team for their hard work. Special recognition goes to John Gemignani for his significant contributions to the improvements in this release and getting the release out of the door. Special thanks goes to collaboration and guidance from Ashutosh Bapat and Serdar Mumcu with John in the path to this release. And a small plug 😁 -- I played a direct part in adding PG18 support as well, more details available in the PR: https://lnkd.in/gW3rUYMG.

At Azure Database for PostgreSQL, we are actively working on bringing this new extension version into both Azure Database for PostgreSQL - flexible server (https://lnkd.in/gz4mq-jF) and Azure HorizonDB (https://lnkd.in/gTh6CV8f). Please stay tuned - more to come! 

For further details, check out the release notes here: https://lnkd.in/gewj7XM6.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to the release of AGE version 1.7.0, a PostgreSQL extension that transforms it into a graph database, and is thanking the open-source team for their hard work, while also mentioning their own contribution to adding PG18 support. The commenter also mentions that Azure Database for PostgreSQL is working on bringing this new extension version to their platforms.

comments.comment_body: John Gemignani Thank you for the recognition john. Really appreciate it.

comments.comment_body: Thank you all for the congrats! I really do appreciate it 😊
comments.post_body: I’m happy to share that I’m starting a new position as Senior Software Engineer at Microsoft!
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to an announcement about starting a new position as Senior Software Engineer at Microsoft, likely offering congratulations or comments on the new role. The discussion is centered around this career update and the commenter's reaction to it.

comments.comment_body: It's better to find out before you get hired than afterwards. I had an interview at Compaq where they were 40 minutes late and one interviewer was pretty insulting with his questions. Like you, I was taught to smile and just get through it. I did, however, sharply correct him on his dismissal of my experience. They called me back and wanted to o hire me but needed me to fill out some preliminary forms.. However, I wasn't keen on working there after that interview. So, I returned their favor for keeping me waiting 40 minutes and said, sure, I'll get you that information to you! I never responded back.
comments.post_body:
The worst interviewer I ever had:

He swaggered into the room and sat down heavily in his chair. “I just have one question for you,” were his first words. “What’s the difference between Java and C++?”

I described some differences, like garbage collection and generics, and the fact that Java is a proprietary language whereas C++ is an ANSI standard, Java compilers generate bytecode, whereas C++ compilers usually generate native machine code, and so on.

“WRONG!” he roared. “There’s no difference! They’re exactly the same language!” Then he sat back, obviously satisfied at having demonstrated his superiority.

I wanted to ask him, “What did you think you would learn about me as a candidate by asking that question?”

Or, “If I accept a job at this company, would I ever have to talk to you again?”

But I just nodded pleasantly and waited for him to pass me to the next interviewer. I did not complain to the company about him. What would be the point? Did I hope to persuade the company to fire him somehow? If I still wanted the job (which is doubtful), would complaining about an interviewer be the best way to make that happen?

If you have a bad feeling about an interviewer, assume that working for that person will be terrible. Politely finish the interview and never look back.

Complaining about an arrogant interviewer has virtually no upside for you, and is much more likely to demonstrate that you’re just as big an asshole as the interviewer was.
comments.context_summary: The author shares a negative interview experience with an arrogant interviewer who claimed Java and C++ are the same language, and advises against complaining about bad interviewers as it's unlikely to have a positive outcome. The author suggests trusting one's instincts and politely ending the interview process if an interviewer raises red flags.

comments.comment_body:
John Gemignani I prefer not to practice the same bad behavior that they did. 

I had another experience (at a different company than in the story above) where I had a bad feeling in the interview, and when I got home I called their HR and just told them as politely as I could that I wasn't considering that job anymore.
